statement: Píratar (Pirate Party) take a process-oriented rather than position-oriented stance on EU membership. The party strongly supported the referendum as an exercise in direct democracy and has advocated for maximum transparency in the pre-referendum information campaign. On substance, the party's EU-relevant positions are mixed: they support the digital single market and free internet governance (aligning with EU initiatives), but oppose EU intellectual property frameworks (particularly software patents and strict copyright enforcement) and are sceptical of EU surveillance and data retention measures. Individual Pirate MPs lean approximately 60–40 towards supporting membership. The party's voter base (predominantly young, urban, tech-savvy) shows approximately 55% EU support.
source_name: Píratar — Policy platform; Althingi committee contributions on EU matters
caveats: The Pirate Party's influence has waned from its peak (14.5% in 2016 elections), and its current parliamentary representation is smaller. The party's decentralised decision-making structure means that official positions may not reflect the views of all members or voters.

related_entries.statement: Vinstrihreyfingin – grænt framboð (Left-Green Movement) and Píratar (Pirate Party) do not have unified positions on EU membership. The Left-Greens are internally divided: some members support membership for environmental and social policy alignment, while others oppose it on sovereignty and democratic grounds. The party's official position favours a referendum but does not take a side. Píratar support direct democracy and have backed the referendum process without endorsing a specific outcome, though individual MPs lean both ways.

EU support varies dramatically by party affiliation. Among Viðreisn voters, ~90% support EU membership; among Samfylkingin voters, ~75%. Píratar voters show ~55% support, and Left-Green voters ~45%. On the opposing side, Sjálfstæðisflokkur voters show ~25% support, Framsóknarflokkur voters ~20%, Miðflokkur voters ~10%, and Flokkur fólksins voters ~10%. These figures (from late 2025 polling) suggest that the referendum will cut across party lines, with significant minorities within each party diverging from the official party position. The ~25% of Independence Party voters who support membership is particularly significant given that party's large vote share (~22% in recent elections).
